title: Quantum Cut Sparsifiers
authors: Arpon Basu, Joshua Brakensiek, Pravesh K. Kothari, Aaron Putterman
abstract: In this paper, we continue a line of research initiated by Basu, Brakensiek, and Putterman [2026] studying the sparsifiability of Hamiltonians. We focus particularly on the sparsifiability of the widely-studied Quantum Cut (QC) Hamiltonians. Our main result is that in an $n$-qubit system, any $n$-qubit QC Hamiltonian can be sparsified to $\widetilde{O}(n /\varepsilon^2)$ many terms while preserving the energy of every state up to a factor of $1 \pm \varepsilon$. Our result can be interpreted as giving an importance sampling scheme for the edges of an arbitrary graph $G$ such that the \emph{Kikuchi} graph at level $\ell$ of the sampled graph is a spectral approximation to the Kikuchi graph of $G$. Importantly, the \emph{same} sampling scheme works simultaneously for all $\ell$.   The natural approach of leverage score sampling, analyzed via matrix concentration inequalities, yields a polynomially worse bound in our setting because the underlying matrices have dimension $\sim 2^n$. Instead, our approach relies on decomposing the action of these matrices into invariant subspaces. Then, by using an operator-valued inequality of Alon and Kozma [Ann. Henri Poincaré, 2020], itself building on an \emph{octopus inequality} of Caputo, Liggett, and Richthammer [J. AMS, 2010], we extend our sparsification technique to all expander graphs. We then invoke expander decomposition to extend our sparsifier to all graphs.
summary: This paper develops methods to create highly efficient 'Quantum Cut sparsifiers' for QC Hamiltonians describing many-body quantum systems. It proves that these complex Hamiltonians can be represented by far fewer terms while maintaining spectral accuracy. This breakthrough has significant implications for the simulation and understanding of large, interacting quantum systems.
main_problem: The primary goal is constructing an efficient, sparse representation (sparsifier) for Quantum Cut (QC) Hamiltonians associated with a general graph G.
main_result: The authors prove the existence of such a sparsifier $	ilde{L}_e^G$ whose size scales as $O(n/\epsilon^2)$ edges, preserving the energy spectrum up to $(1 \pm \epsilon)$.
method: The technique relies on decomposing the action of the Hamiltonians into invariant subspaces and employing advanced operator-valued inequalities (like Alon-Kozma's) derived from harmonic analysis on hypercube slices.
model_or_system: The system is defined by QC Hamiltonians, $L_G$, which are related to the Laplacian of a graph called the Kikuchi Graph ($KG$). These operators act on function spaces over subsets of vertices in an $n$-qubit system.
key_observables: Spectral approximation error ($\epsilon$), the size of the sparsifier $|E'|$, and the spectral gap relative to the full Hamiltonian.
important_parameters: The number of qubits ($n$) and the desired accuracy ($\epsilon$).
main_assumptions: Success relies on decomposing graphs into 'mild expanders' and utilizing advanced inequalities that overcome limitations faced by standard matrix concentration methods when local term importance is high.
paper_structure: The paper builds conceptually from classical cut-sparsifiers to the quantum setting, establishing the connection to Kikuchi graphs. The methodology involves spectral decomposition, applying advanced inequalities (Alon-Kozma), and generalizing results from complete/expander graphs to arbitrary weighted graphs via expander decomposition.
why_interesting: This work provides a powerful tool for analyzing complex quantum Hamiltonians by showing they can be accurately approximated using only a sparse set of interactions, which is crucial for simulating large quantum systems efficiently.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
